It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

Looking at the new level:
- There's a vertical bar structure in the center
- A pattern on the left (yellow dots on gray background)
- A grid on the right (white and gray cells)
- Green timer on the right edge
Let me analyze the new level structure.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Level 2 - analyze the new structure
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("All nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    r_min = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
    c_min = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
    r_max = max(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
    c_max = max(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, ({r_min},{c_min})-({r_max},{c_max}), children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Level 2 structure:
1. Top bar: blue square (b, 14px) at (10,30)-(14,35) with light blue (S, 16px) inside at (11,31)-(14,34)
2. Vertical structure below: gray (g, 194px) at (15,27)-(38,38) containing:
   - 6 yellow dots (Y, 1px each) at positions forming an X pattern
   - 2 charcoal blocks (c, 6px each) at (27,30)-(30,31) and (27,34)-(30,35)
   - Blue (b, 8px) at (35,31)-(36,34) and light blue (S, 8px) at (37,31)-(38,34) - this is the bottom end of the bar
3. Yellow dots outside the gray structure: (23,30) and (23,35)
4. Grid (G, 208px) at (47,22)-(63,38):
   - Row 0: W, W, g (white, white, gray)
   - Row 1: g, W, g (gray, white, gray)
   - Row 2: g, g, g (gray, gray, gray)
5. Pattern box (G, 36px) at (50,11)-(59,20):
   - Contains yellow dots forming a pattern
   - Yellow: (51,12)-(52,13), (51,15)-(52,16), (54,15)-(55,16)
   - This forms an L-shape or similar
So the pattern to stamp is the yellow L-shape (3 cells). The grid has 2 white cells already.
The bar is now VERTICAL. The top is the blue square, the bottom is the blue+light blue end. The yellow X pattern is on the bar.
I think the mechanic is similar:
- UP/DOWN moves the bar's end along the bar
- LEFT/RIGHT moves the bar's end to the left/right extremes
- Clicking the pattern stamps it on the grid
- Pressing LEFT when the bar's end is at the leftmost position completes the level
Let me check: the bar's end is at the bottom (rows 35-38). I need to move it to the top, then press LEFT to complete.
Wait, but in Level 1, I had to move the bar's right end all the way to the LEFT, then press LEFT. The bar was horizontal.
In Level 2, the bar is vertical. So maybe I need to move the bar's end all the way UP, then press UP? Or move it LEFT and press LEFT?
Let me first understand the bar structure better. The bar has:
- Top: blue square (b) at (10,30)-(14,35)
- Middle: gray shaft with yellow X pattern
- Bottom: blue (b) at (35,31)-(36,34) + light blue (S) at (37,31)-(38,34)
The bottom end is the "movable" part. In Level 1, LEFT/RIGHT moved the right end along the horizontal bar. Here, UP/DOWN might move the bottom end along the vertical bar.
Let me try pressing UP to move the bottom end upward.
